title: Choosing products with recycled content
subtitle: What recycled content labels mean and which categories have the most material impact
excerpt: Recycled content in a product means that a proportion of the raw material came from post-consumer or post-industrial waste rather than virgin extraction. The carbon saving varies substantially by material: recycled aluminium uses 95% less energy than virgin; recycled plastic uses 70-80% less; recycled steel uses 55-65% less.
sourceNotes: WRAP guidance on recycled content standards and labelling. Aluminium Stewardship Initiative data on recycled aluminium energy savings. Ellen MacArthur Foundation circular economy metrics on recycled content impact.

glossaryTerms.definition: Material that has been used by a consumer and then recycled, as distinct from pre-consumer (or post-industrial) recycled material, which is manufacturing offcuts and waste that never reached a consumer. PCR content has greater circular economy value because it diverts material from waste streams.

Recycling a material into a lower-grade product that cannot itself be recycled further. For example, mixed-colour plastics recycled into black plastic garden furniture. Contrasted with closed-loop recycling (aluminium, steel, glass) where the recycled material is equivalent to virgin.

content.text: The most impactful categories for buying

content are those where: (a) the carbon saving from recycled vs virgin is large, (b) the products are large or frequently replaced, and (c) recycled-content alternatives are readily available at similar prices.

Recycled aluminium uses approximately 95% less energy than virgin aluminium. Look for cookware, food storage containers, and packaging with stated recycled content. Aluminium is a closed-loop material: it can be recycled indefinitely without quality loss. Buying aluminium products and returning them for recycling at end-of-life is more effective than avoiding aluminium entirely.
Recycled plastic (rPET, rHDPE) in clothing (fleece from bottles), garden products, and packaging reduces virgin plastic demand. Most recycled plastic is

rather than closed-loop, so the chain eventually reaches end-of-life landfill or incineration, but the life extension still reduces primary extraction. Look for the percentage recycled content on garden furniture, decking, and outdoor products: 50-100% recycled content is available in most of these categories.

Recycled paper and cardboard uses 25-50% less energy than virgin-fibre paper and avoids the need for virgin forest fibre. FSC (Forest Stewardship Council) and PEFC certification on virgin-fibre paper indicates sustainably managed forestry; recycled content avoids the forest impact entirely. Office paper, notebooks, and packaging are the main categories where recycled-content products are available at the same price as virgin alternatives.
